content.text: A service blueprint for a GenAI touchpoint extends the classic blueprint format with rows that do not exist in traditional service design: the AI model layer, a safety/guardrails layer, and an observability layer. These additions are not cosmetic — they expose the invisible decisions and risks that determine whether the product is safe, reliable, and auditable.

attrs.alt: Service Blueprint — GenAI Document Review Feature

content.text: A standard service blueprint has three lanes divided by two lines: the Line of Visibility (what users see vs. what happens backstage) and the Line of Interaction (user actions vs. system responses). GenAI blueprints add three backstage rows below the line of visibility:
What the user does at each phase
Where confidence signals, citations, and human-gate UIs live
Prompt construction, chunking, context assembly, streaming
Most GenAI latency and quality bugs originate here
LLM inference, RAG retrieval, tool calls, model version
Where capability limits and cost are determined
Input/output filters, PII detection, safety classifiers, rate limits
Prevents the blueprint from shipping unsafe behaviours
Blob storage, vector DB, audit tables, caches
Determines what can be retrieved, replayed, or audited
Metrics, traces, and logs collected per phase
Makes the invisible AI behaviour measurable after deployment

separates what users see (Frontend) from what they cannot (AI Orchestration downward). For GenAI products, this line is the trust boundary: everything above it must communicate uncertainty honestly because everything below it is opaque to the user.

(drawn between AI Orchestration and Model Layer in the blueprint above) marks where orchestration code hands off to the model. This is important because the orchestration layer — not the model — controls prompt construction, context size, retrieval strategy, and streaming behaviour. Problems in these areas are fixable in engineering; model capability limits are not.
The Frontend row documents every UI affordance that communicates AI behaviour to the user. Common GenAI-specific components:
Which step is running; estimated time remaining
Model certainty for each finding; threshold for low-confidence flag
Source document and page/chunk for each factual claim
Which findings are auto-approved vs. require review; why
Partial output appears as tokens arrive; stable layout during stream
User's review decisions are recorded and accessible

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class OrchestrationStep:
    """One cell in the AI Orchestration row."""
    phase:          str
    actions:        list[str]
    latency_budget_ms: int     # target P95 latency for this step
    context_tokens: int = 0    # tokens sent to model (for cost estimation)
    streaming:      bool = False

    def estimated_cost_usd(self, cost_per_1m_tokens: float = 3.0) -> float:
        return (self.context_tokens / 1_000_000) * cost_per_1m_tokens

DOCUMENT_REVIEW_ORCHESTRATION = [
    OrchestrationStep(
        phase="Upload",
        actions=["Validate file type and size", "Store to blob", "Enqueue analysis job"],
        latency_budget_ms=500,
    ),
    OrchestrationStep(
        phase="AI Analysis",
        actions=[
            "Chunk document into 512-token segments",
            "Retrieve relevant prior clauses from vector DB",
            "Build system prompt with document context",
            "Call LLM with streaming enabled",
        ],
        latency_budget_ms=8000,
        context_tokens=12000,
        streaming=True,
    ),
    OrchestrationStep(
        phase="Review Output",
        actions=[
            "Parse structured findings from LLM response",
            "Attach confidence scores",
            "Map findings to source chunks for citation",
        ],
        latency_budget_ms=300,
    ),
    OrchestrationStep(
        phase="Human Gate",
        actions=["Write human decision to audit_log", "Update finding status"],
        latency_budget_ms=200,
    ),
    OrchestrationStep(
        phase="Export",
        actions=["Render PDF with audit trail", "PII strip pass", "Sign with timestamp"],
        latency_budget_ms=2000,
    ),
]
The Guardrails row is the most frequently omitted in early blueprints. It documents the safety controls that run per-phase, not just as a single check before or after the model call.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class GuardrailResult(str, Enum):
    PASS    = "pass"
    BLOCK   = "block"
    REDACT  = "redact"
    ESCALATE= "escalate"

@dataclass
class GuardrailSpec:
    phase:       str
    name:        str
    target:      str          # "input" | "output" | "file"
    action:      GuardrailResult
    description: str

DOCUMENT_REVIEW_GUARDRAILS = [
    GuardrailSpec(
        phase="Upload",
        name="malware_scan",
        target="file",
        action=GuardrailResult.BLOCK,
        description="Reject files that fail antivirus signature check",
    ),
    GuardrailSpec(
        phase="AI Analysis",
        name="pii_input_filter",
        target="input",
        action=GuardrailResult.REDACT,
        description="Replace PII (names, emails, account numbers) in document chunks before sending to LLM",
    ),
    GuardrailSpec(
        phase="AI Analysis",
        name="prompt_injection_detector",
        target="input",
        action=GuardrailResult.BLOCK,
        description="Block document chunks that contain prompt-injection patterns",
    ),
    GuardrailSpec(
        phase="Review Output",
        name="output_toxicity_classifier",
        target="output",
        action=GuardrailResult.ESCALATE,
        description="Route findings with toxicity score > 0.8 to human review regardless of confidence",
    ),
    GuardrailSpec(
        phase="Human Gate",
        name="high_harm_auto_approve_block",
        target="output",
        action=GuardrailResult.BLOCK,
        description="Prevent automatic approval of findings tagged harm_severity >= significant",
    ),
    GuardrailSpec(
        phase="Export",
        name="pii_output_strip",
        target="output",
        action=GuardrailResult.REDACT,
        description="Strip any PII that survived earlier filters from the exported report",
    ),
]

The Observability row turns the blueprint into a testable specification. Each metric listed in this row has a phase, an expected value, and an alert threshold. Without this row, engineers must decide ad-hoc which signals to instrument, and critical signals (hallucination rate, human override rate) are often missed.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ObservabilitySpec:
    phase:         str
    metric_name:   str
    type:          str      # "counter" | "histogram" | "gauge"
    unit:          str
    alert_threshold: str    # condition that pages on-call
    purpose:       str

DOCUMENT_REVIEW_OBSERVABILITY = [
    ObservabilitySpec(
        phase="Upload",
        metric_name="upload_latency_ms",
        type="histogram",
        unit="milliseconds",
        alert_threshold="P95 > 1000ms",
        purpose="Detect CDN or storage latency spikes",
    ),
    ObservabilitySpec(
        phase="AI Analysis",
        metric_name="llm_latency_ms",
        type="histogram",
        unit="milliseconds",
        alert_threshold="P95 > 12000ms",
        purpose="Detect model provider degradation",
    ),
    ObservabilitySpec(
        phase="AI Analysis",
        metric_name="token_count_per_doc",
        type="histogram",
        unit="tokens",
        alert_threshold="P99 > 20000",
        purpose="Catch unexpectedly large documents that will exceed context window",
    ),
    ObservabilitySpec(
        phase="Review Output",
        metric_name="confidence_p50",
        type="gauge",
        unit="score 0-1",
        alert_threshold="7-day rolling mean < 0.70",
        purpose="Detect model quality degradation over time",
    ),
    ObservabilitySpec(
        phase="Review Output",
        metric_name="hallucination_rate",
        type="gauge",
        unit="fraction",
        alert_threshold="Weekly rate > 0.05",
        purpose="Trigger model evaluation cycle if hallucination increases",
    ),
    ObservabilitySpec(
        phase="Human Gate",
        metric_name="human_review_rate",
        type="gauge",
        unit="fraction of findings",
        alert_threshold="Rate < 0.01 for 24h (possible gate bypass)",
        purpose="Ensure human gate is exercised; detect circumvention",
    ),
    ObservabilitySpec(
        phase="Human Gate",
        metric_name="override_rate",
        type="gauge",
        unit="fraction of reviewed findings",
        alert_threshold="Weekly rate > 0.20 (AI accuracy problem)",
        purpose="High override rate signals prompt or retrieval issue",
    ),
]

metric — the fraction of AI findings that a human reviewer rejects — is the most actionable quality signal in a GenAI blueprint. A rising override rate points directly to a problem in the Model Layer or AI Orchestration row, not the frontend.
The service blueprint is not a one-time design artefact. It should be reviewed at three points in the delivery lifecycle:
Add new phases or guardrails from job stories
Updated blueprint PDF in sprint ticket
Revise orchestration and observability rows based on implementation constraints
Verify guardrails row is fully implemented; check observability metrics emit
Comparing Blueprint Phases to Sprint Tickets

A service blueprint phase maps directly to one or more sprint tickets. The table below shows how the document-review blueprint translates into a ticket hierarchy. This translation makes the blueprint actionable immediately in sprint planning without a separate grooming step.
File drag-drop UI; malware scan integration; blob storage write; upload_latency_ms metric
Chunking strategy; vector DB retrieval; prompt template; streaming; llm_latency_ms + token_count metrics
Highlighted PDF viewer; confidence badge component; citation link; hallucination_rate metric
Approve/reject UI; audit_log write; high-harm block guardrail; override_rate + human_review_rate metrics
PDF renderer; audit trail embed; PII strip; export_latency_ms metric
Definition of Done per Blueprint Row

Teams that use a blueprint as their primary design artefact can derive a Definition of Done per row rather than per ticket, ensuring cross-cutting concerns (especially guardrails and observability) are not deferred.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RowDefinitionOfDone:
    row_name:  str
    must_haves: list[str]

BLUEPRINT_DOD = [
    RowDefinitionOfDone(
        row_name="Frontend",
        must_haves=[
            "All confidence signals render correctly at P05, P50, P95 confidence values",
            "Human gate UI tested with high-harm and low-harm findings",
            "Streaming render shows no layout shift during token arrival",
            "Citation links open source chunk at correct position",
        ]
    ),
    RowDefinitionOfDone(
        row_name="AI Orchestration",
        must_haves=[
            "Latency budget (P95) met in staging load test",
            "Chunking strategy tested with documents at min and max size limits",
            "Prompt template version pinned and logged with each inference call",
        ]
    ),
    RowDefinitionOfDone(
        row_name="Guardrails",
        must_haves=[
            "PII input filter tested with synthetic PII dataset (names, emails, IDs)",
            "Prompt injection detector tested with OWASP top injection patterns",
            "High-harm block tested: auto-approve button disabled for harm≥significant",
            "PII output strip verified on report export",
        ]
    ),
    RowDefinitionOfDone(
        row_name="Observability",
        must_haves=[
            "All blueprint metrics appear in staging dashboard",
            "Alert rules configured and smoke-tested (confirmed they fire on threshold breach)",
            "override_rate and hallucination_rate visible in weekly quality report",
        ]
    ),
]

def all_dod_passed(results: dict[str, bool]) -> bool:
    """results maps (row_name, must_have) -> passed."""
    return all(results.values())

Include the Guardrails and Observability rows in the Definition of Done explicitly. Both are invisible to the user and are routinely deferred to 'hardening sprints' that never happen. Treating them as must-have in the DoD is the only reliable way to ensure they ship with the feature.

Service blueprints for GenAI touchpoints extend the classic format with AI Orchestration, Model Layer, Guardrails, and Observability rows. These rows document the invisible behaviour that determines safety, latency, and auditability.
Draw the Line of Visibility to separate the frontend (user-facing confidence signals, citations, human gates) from the backstage (orchestration, model, guardrails).

The Guardrails row documents per-phase safety controls — not just a single post-processing check. Input PII filter, prompt injection detection, output toxicity classifier, and high-harm auto-approve block are four distinct rows in four distinct phases.
The Observability row converts the blueprint into a testable specification. Each metric has a phase, an expected value, and an alert threshold.
The override_rate metric (fraction of AI findings rejected by human reviewers) is the most actionable quality signal — rising override rate points to orchestration or model layer problems.
Use the blueprint as a pre-release checklist: all guardrails implemented, all metrics emitting, latency budgets verified, human gate tested end-to-end.

A service blueprint authored before sprint planning prevents the most costly class of GenAI production incidents: invisible safety controls that were assumed to exist but were never explicitly specified.
